tasks.title: Fathom auto-ingest on cron — calls arrive and process while nobody is logged in (the 'Gmail' behaviour)
tasks.description: Ivan 2026-07-16: 'I would like it to be like gmail, receiving mail (and in this case processing the data) even when I am not actively logged in... Ivan or Gabriel can log in and see the processed calls.' Today the cron fires every 5 min and does nothing (pollFathom is a stub), so NOTHING arrives while logged out. Wire the cron to the Fathom client built in TASK-035: pull new calls since the cursor, import them, and generate — all server-side, no session.
acceptance_criteria: Cron imports new Fathom calls with no browser session open
Newly imported calls are auto-generated so they are Ready when someone logs in
First-run cursor is seeded at key-save time — the cron must NEVER implicitly backfill history
Auto-generation is bounded to genuinely new calls only (never a backfill), so cost stays proportional to real call volume
No duplicate calls across polls (dedupe on Fathom recording_id)
Every cron run logs what it did — imported, generated, skipped, failed (TASK-040)
A cron failure never wedges the cursor or loses calls
Both Ivan and Gabriel see identical processed state on login (already true — single shared account + server-side data)
testing_criteria: Simulate a poll against real Fathom API and confirm call rows created
Run poll twice; assert no duplicates
Assert calls route to the correct account
Test API-failure path
Simulate a first run with no prior cursor: assert it does NOT import the entire Fathom history
notes: Currently a stub: src/index.js:286 logs 'Fathom polling not yet implemented'. Cron trigger (*/5 * * * *) is already registered and firing harmlessly. Will likely need a new migration to track last-polled cursor / external recording id for dedupe. This is THE feature that eliminates Gabriel's copy/paste — highest user value of the remaining work. FIRST-RUN CURSOR TRAP (raised by Ivan 2026-07-16, 'there are calls from today'): a poller keyed on 'fetch since last_polled_at' has no cursor on first run. Defaulting to now = today's calls are silently lost. Defaulting to epoch = it backfills Gabriel's ENTIRE Fathom history and — because createCall() auto-processes — fires an LLM run per call, unattended. At ~55 calls/wk over a year that is ~2,800 calls and plausibly $300-400 in one cron tick. DECISION: seed the cursor to now at key-save time; the cron only ever picks up genuinely new calls. Historical import is a separate, explicit, user-driven flow (TASK-033). RESCOPED 2026-07-16 to Ivan's 'Gmail' requirement. NOTE the sequencing: auto-generation must NOT be switched on before TASK-015 — not one real LLM generation has ever succeeded, and turning on an unattended cron that fires 4 paid calls per arriving call, with an unproven generation path, would fail silently on every call and could burn money doing it. Prove one manual generation first. Now also depends on TASK-040 so cron activity is observable — an unattended job with no log is undebuggable by definition. REASSESSED 2026-07-16: this task assumed waitUntil already gave us background processing. It never did (30s cap, TASK-045). The Gmail behaviour depends on TASK-045's Workflow, not on the cron alone. IMPLEMENTED 2026-07-17, unblocked by TASK-015's first successful run. Ivan's need: Gabriel records early tomorrow, Ivan checks midday, and he wanted calls already processed. Clarified a misconception worth keeping: closing the laptop was NEVER the issue - the app is server-side on Cloudflare. The reason nothing arrived is that pollFathom was a literal stub (console.log('not yet implemented')). DESIGN: bounded 12h lookback window + the unique (account_id, external_id) index, rather than a stored cursor - a corrupt/reset cursor could replay history, a 12h window structurally cannot. MAX_AUTO_PROCESS_PER_TICK=3 bounds the blast radius; overflow is imported and logged, NOT dropped, so the cap delays spend rather than losing calls. Transcript-less meetings are SKIPPED not imported - importing one would let external_id dedupe permanently strand it empty once Fathom finished transcribing. The cron only generates calls IT imported this tick, so a manually-pasted call left 'new' on purpose is never auto-spent. scheduled() now awaits instead of ctx.waitUntil (30s cap, TASK-045).

SUPERSEDED-IN-PART 2026-07-17 by TASK-058: the cron still imports, but auto-PROCESSING is OFF by default (Fathom captures internal meetings too). Import half stands; auto-generate half is gated behind AUTO_PROCESS_IMPORTS=false.

2026-07-17 per-tick cap: 7 arriving calls -> all 7 imported, only 3 generations launched, 'deferred 4' logged so the truncation is visible rather than silent - pass
2026-07-17 transcript-less meeting skipped, then imported correctly on a later tick once the transcript landed (proves it is not stranded by dedupe) - pass
2026-07-17 oldest-first generation order so Gabriel reads them in the order he made them - pass
2026-07-17 Fathom 503, 401, and an unexpected response shape each log a distinct error and do not throw; a shape mismatch is NOT reported as 'no calls' - pass
2026-07-17 a manually-pasted call left in 'new' is never picked up by the cron - pass

tasks.title: Prompt caching: cache the transcript as a shared prefix across the 4 per-call requests
tasks.description: Ivan asked (2026-07-16) whether prompt caching would lower API costs. VERIFIED: yes, but it requires restructuring, not just adding cache_control. Today generateOutputs() sends the transcript FOUR times per call (1 debrief + 3 tone variants) and sets no cache_control anywhere. Caching is a strict prefix match and the transcript currently sits at the END of every prompt behind differing instructions, so the 4 requests share no cacheable prefix at all. Fix: invert to transcript-first as content blocks, with cache_control on the transcript block, so the awaited debrief writes the cache and the 3 parallel tone calls read it.
acceptance_criteria: Transcript is the first content block of all 4 requests and is byte-identical across them
cache_control {type: ephemeral} sits on the transcript block (5-min TTL default, NOT 1h)
usage.cache_creation_input_tokens is non-zero on the debrief request
usage.cache_read_input_tokens is non-zero on all 3 tone requests
Measured input tokens per call drop materially vs the pre-change baseline
Debrief still completes BEFORE the tone calls fire (the awaited ordering is what makes the cache readable)
Master prompt stays OUT of the cached prefix (it is only used by the debrief; caching it into the tone calls costs more and pollutes their context)
testing_criteria: Process a real transcript; assert cache_creation_input_tokens > 0 on request 1 and cache_read_input_tokens > 0 on requests 2-4
Compare measured total input tokens per call before vs after the change (must go DOWN, not up)
Test a short transcript (< ~1024 tokens): confirm it silently does not cache and the run still succeeds
Confirm output quality is unchanged — reordering transcript-before-instructions must not degrade the debrief or the drafts
Measure wall-clock time before/after (cache reads should cut prefill latency — Gabriel's #1 complaint is waiting)
tasks.notes: VERIFIED against current Anthropic docs 2026-07-16. Mechanics: cache write = 1.25x base input (5-min TTL), read = 0.1x; Sonnet 5 minimum cacheable prefix = 1024 tokens (a typical 29-41 min transcript is ~5-8k tokens, so it clears easily). Use the 5-min TTL — the debrief-to-tone gap is seconds, and 1h TTL doubles the write cost for zero benefit. ESTIMATED SAVING (no real measurements exist yet — the app has never made a real API call): ~58% off input tokens, which at Gabriel's ~55 calls/week is roughly $8/mo now (Sonnet 5 intro pricing) or ~$12/mo after the intro rate ends 2026-08-31. Total bill is only ~$28-42/mo either way, so this is a real but SMALL absolute win — priority medium, not high. The latency win may matter more than the cost win. TRAP 1 (why this depends on TASK-015): a broken prefix and an under-length prompt both fail SILENTLY with no error. The ONLY proof it works is non-zero usage.cache_read_input_tokens, which needs a real API key. Do not mark complete on 'the code looks right'. TRAP 2: if caching is wired up wrong, it costs MORE than today — every request pays the 1.25x write premium and nothing ever reads. TRAP 3 (load-bearing ordering): this only works because the debrief is awaited before Promise.all fires the tone calls. A cache entry is only readable after the first response begins. Anyone who later 'optimizes' by firing all 4 in parallel will turn 3 cache reads into 3 cache writes and silently raise the bill. Comment this in the code. BIGGER LEVER, NOT THIS TASK: output tokens are ~half the bill and caching cannot touch them. The 9-section debrief dominates output. Trimming it would cut cost AND address Gabriel's own complaint ('it's just so much, and I don't always read all of it') — that is the deferred v2 decision in NEXT_STEPS decision #3, not this task. REAL DATA 2026-07-16 (supersedes the estimates above): the first real Fathom call is a 62-minute call with a 75,655-char transcript ~= 19,000 tokens — roughly 2.7x the 7,000-token figure used in the original estimate. Recomputed: current input ~78k tokens/call (19k x 4); with transcript-prefix caching ~31k/call, i.e. ~60% reduction and roughly $20-25/mo saved at ~238 calls/mo (intro pricing), not the ~$8 originally estimated. Caching is now materially more worthwhile than first assessed, and the latency win is larger too. Re-baseline again once a few more real calls land — 62 min may be long-tail, not typical. SUPERSEDED-PENDING 2026-07-16: Ivan asked why the transcript is sent 4x. TASK-042 removes the duplication at the source rather than discounting it. Caching 4 copies (~31k effective) is strictly worse than not sending 3 of them (~29k actual), and once TASK-042 lands the debrief is the only request carrying the transcript — a lone request has no sibling to share a cached prefix with, so caching would buy nothing. Do not start this task; resolve TASK-042 first, then close this one as superseded unless a new multi-request-per-transcript path appears. CLOSED AS SUPERSEDED 2026-07-16: TASK-042 landed. The transcript is now sent once, so there is no repeated prefix left to cache. Real run confirms 37k input for the whole generation.
test_results: 2026-07-16 Closed as superseded by TASK-042, not implemented. Caching 4 copies is strictly worse than not sending 3 of them.

tasks.title: Generation must survive navigating away + persist processing/failed state
tasks.description: Ivan 2026-07-16: 'took a long time to generate. I don't know if I clicked out too soon and it stopped the generation.' CONFIRMED — processCall() awaits all 4 LLM calls inline in the HTTP request and ctx is never passed to it, so a client disconnect can cancel the work. processed_at is only written at the very end, so a killed run leaves the call indistinguishable from 'New' with no error recorded. There is also no guard against starting a second run while one is in flight (double spend). Fix: persist a processing state machine, run generation via ctx.waitUntil so it completes regardless of the client, return immediately, and let the UI poll.
acceptance_criteria: calls carry an explicit processing_status: new | processing | processed | failed

Existing processed calls are backfilled to 'processed' by the migration
testing_criteria: Start generation, immediately abort the HTTP request client-side; assert the call still reaches 'processed'
Fire two concurrent process requests; assert exactly one generation runs (count LLM attempts)
Force a failure (bad key); assert status='failed' and the error is recorded and shown

Assert a call stuck in 'processing' past the stale window can be retried

Cloudflare cancels request-scoped work on client disconnect unless it is handed to ctx.waitUntil; ctx is currently available in fetch() but never threaded through route() to processCall.

tasks.title: Generation takes 10+ min and dies silently — Sonnet 5 runs adaptive thinking at high effort by default

tasks.title: Stop sending the 19k-token transcript 4x per call — the drafting calls need the debrief's facts, not the raw transcript
tasks.description: Ivan 2026-07-16: 'Why are we running a 19,000-token transcript, four times over?' Fair question; there is no good answer. generateOutputs() interpolates ${call.transcript} into the debrief request AND into each of the 3 parallel tone requests = ~77k input tokens per call. The 3-way split was a deliberate SPEED choice (code comment: 'the v1.5 speed fix') so the tone slider switches instantly, and it was sound against the 7k-token transcript estimate. At the real 19k it inverts: we pay 4 prefills of 19k, and prefill is the dominant latency at that size (a likely co-contributor to the TASK-041 hang). The deeper flaw: the tone calls never needed the transcript. The debrief has already read it and extracted outcome, client name, objections, next steps and rapport details — the tone prompts even receive parsed.outcome, which proves we were already holding the answer while re-sending the transcript so the model could re-derive it.
acceptance_criteria: The transcript is sent to the LLM EXACTLY ONCE per call (the debrief). Assert this directly, not by inspection.

The 3 tone calls are fed a compact distillation from the debrief JSON (outcome, client name, objections + any verbatim client quotes worth mirroring, next steps, rapport details) instead of the transcript
Measured input tokens per call drop from ~77k to under ~30k on the real 19k-token transcript
All 3 tone variants are still generated up front — instant tone switching is a deliberate UX property and must not regress
Draft quality is not worse: the SMS/email must still reference call-specific specifics, not generic filler
The debrief JSON is checked to actually carry everything the drafts need; if a field is missing, ADD it to the debrief prompt rather than falling back to the transcript
testing_criteria: Stub fetch; assert the transcript string appears in exactly 1 of the 4 outgoing request bodies
Log real input_tokens before vs after on the same real call; assert the drop and record both numbers
Side-by-side the drafts before vs after on the same real transcript; a human (Ivan or Gabriel) confirms specificity did not degrade
Measure wall-clock before vs after — killing 57k tokens of prefill should be FASTER despite fewer parallel prefills
Assert a missing/short debrief field does not silently produce a generic draft
tasks.notes: MEASURED, not estimated: transcript = 75,655 chars ~= 19k tokens. Today ~77.2k input tokens/call (19k debrief + 1.2k master + 3 x 19k) ~= $0.154/call input, ~$37/mo at ~238 calls/mo. OPTIONS COSTED: (1) RECOMMENDED — drafting calls get the debrief's facts (~3k) instead of the transcript: ~29k/call, -62%, ~$14/mo. Keeps the parallel fan-out, keeps instant tone switching, small blast radius, and is arguably BETTER quality because the drafts get curated facts rather than 62 minutes of raw dialogue to re-mine. (2) One call returns debrief + all 3 tones: ~20k/call, -74%, ~$10/mo. Closest to Gabriel's original workflow (his master prompt section 7 already asks for the follow-up text/email in the same pass) but a bigger rewrite, one malformed JSON loses everything, more truncation risk against max_tokens 16k, and no parallelism. (3) Generate only the AI-suggested tone up front, others on demand — cheapest of all but breaks the instant-switch UX Ivan specifically designed. RELATIONSHIP TO TASK-031 (prompt caching): this SUPERSEDES most of its value. Caching made 4 x 19k cheaper (~31k effective); this makes the 4th, 3rd and 2nd copies not exist (~29k actual). Do THIS first — it is a real fix, whereas caching is a discount on waste. If both ship, caching would then only cover the debrief's own 19k, which is a single request with nothing to share a prefix with, i.e. worthless. Expect TASK-031 to be closed as superseded once this lands. Do not implement before TASK-015: changing what the drafts are fed is unverifiable while no real generation has ever succeeded, and 'quality did not degrade' has no baseline to compare against. IMPLEMENTED 2026-07-16 at Ivan's direction, ahead of its TASK-015 dependency (he chose to test the real run afterward). Consequence to be honest about: the cost/token claim is verified by stubbed request bodies and is solid, but the QUALITY claim is not - there has never been a successful real run, so there is no before/after to compare drafts against. If the drafts come back thin, the fix is to enrich the debrief's followUp fields, NOT to put the transcript back.

tasks.title: ROOT CAUSE of the silent 10+ min hangs: non-streaming Anthropic request holds an idle connection and gets dropped
tasks.description: Ivan 2026-07-16, second hang: 12.6 min, still 'processing', 0 outputs, NO error logged. The per-step logging from TASK-041 localised it — generation.started fires, generation.debrief_done never does, so it dies inside the FIRST call (the debrief). TASK-041's thinking/effort fix did not cure it and TASK-042 could not (it only shrank calls 2-4). VERIFIED ROOT CAUSE in the Anthropic docs: we call fetch() directly with stream:false and max_tokens:16000. A non-streaming request sends ZERO bytes until the entire message is generated, so the connection sits completely idle for minutes. Docs, verbatim: '504 timeout_error: The request timed out while processing. Consider using the streaming Messages API for long-running requests'; 'Avoid setting a large max_tokens value without using the streaming Messages API: Some networks may drop idle connections after a VARIABLE period of time, which can cause the request to fail or time out WITHOUT RECEIVING A RESPONSE from Anthropic'; and 'The SDKs validate that your non-streaming requests are not expected to exceed a 10-minute timeout. They also set a socket option for TCP keep-alive.' We use raw fetch, so we get NONE of those protections. 'Variable period' explains 18 min the first time and 12+ the second. The dropped connection never resolves or rejects, so the catch block never runs and nothing is ever logged — the silence IS the symptom.
acceptance_criteria: Every Anthropic request sets stream:true and the SSE event stream is accumulated into the same {text, usage} shape complete() returns today
Bytes flow continuously for the whole generation, so the connection is never idle long enough to be dropped
usage is captured from the stream: input_tokens/cache_* from message_start, output_tokens from message_delta (CUMULATIVE per the docs — assign, do not add)
stop_reason is read from message_delta; max_tokens and refusal still raise the same explicit errors as before
Mid-stream `event: error` (e.g. overloaded_error, which arrives AFTER a 200) is detected and raised — it must not be silently swallowed as a short response
An overall request timeout aborts and THROWS, so a hang can never again present as an unbounded spinner with no log entry
A hang or drop now produces a generation.failed row with real error text
testing_criteria: Stub fetch with a real SSE byte stream (chunked mid-event, ping events interleaved, split across reads) and assert the accumulated text and usage exactly match the old non-streaming result
Assert every outgoing body has stream:true
Assert a mid-stream error event throws rather than returning truncated text
Assert stop_reason:max_tokens from message_delta still throws the truncation error
Assert an SSE event split across two read() chunks is parsed correctly (buffer boundary handling)
Assert the timeout aborts and throws rather than hanging
tasks.notes: THIS IS WHY BOTH REAL RUNS DIED. TASK-041 fixed a real bug (adaptive thinking at high effort) but was diagnosed as THE cause on the strength of one killed run — it was a contributing cause at most. Lesson recorded honestly: 'I found a bug that explains the symptom' is not 'I found the bug'. The tell I under-weighted both times: a Worker killed by a platform limit and a fetch that never settles look IDENTICAL from the log (started, silence, no throw) — and I twice read that silence as 'Worker killed' without checking the API's own documented failure mode. Streaming also makes a REAL progress bar possible (TASK-044) because streamed bytes are genuine progress rather than a fake animation. Do NOT 'simplify' this back to stream:false: max_tokens is 16000 and a 9-section debrief takes minutes to generate, which is exactly the documented drop scenario. HONESTY CONSTRAINT: the percentage is an estimate (streamed chars / expected chars), and it must be built so it CANNOT advance without real bytes arriving. tasks.description: Ivan 2026-07-16, fourth report of a stuck run. This time the streaming fix (TASK-043) gave us the evidence that was missing all along: processing_progress reached 33 and then FROZE, and the 5-minute AbortSignal.timeout never fired. A timer that never fires means the isolate is gone. VERIFIED against Cloudflare's limits doc, verbatim: 'waitUntil() can extend execution for up to 30 seconds after the response is sent or the client disconnects.' Our design is exactly the pathological case: startProcessing() returns 202 IMMEDIATELY and then calls ctx.waitUntil(runGeneration) — which buys 30 seconds for a job that takes minutes. THE ARITHMETIC CONFIRMS IT EXACTLY: 33% of the bar = 33/70 of the debrief share = ~6,600 chars; Sonnet 5 streams ~220 chars/sec; 6,600/220 = 30 SECONDS. The Worker died at precisely the documented limit. FIX: Cloudflare Workflows — per-step wall-clock is UNLIMITED ('waiting on network I/O calls or querying a database' does not count), only CPU is capped at 30s (raisable to 5 min), and our work is ~100% I/O wait on Anthropic. Workflows also genuinely survives nobody being logged in, which waitUntil never did.

The cheap D1 save step MAY retry, since it costs nothing
Progress still streams to processing_progress from inside the step
A failed workflow marks the call 'failed' with real error text rather than leaving it wedged in 'processing' forever
The 'safe to close this tab' promise in the UI is either true or removed

Assert the LLM step is configured with retries limit 0 (money-bomb guard) and prove it via the step config, not by reading the code

REAL RUN with the tab closed immediately after clicking Generate
Confirm progress advances past 33% — the exact point every previous run died

tasks.notes: FOURTH diagnosis of this symptom, and the first one supported by arithmetic rather than plausibility. The three before it: TASK-041 (adaptive thinking) - a REAL bug, not this. TASK-042 (4x transcript) - a REAL waste, not this. TASK-043 (non-streaming idle drop) - a REAL hazard, not this, BUT it is what made this diagnosable: without streamed progress there was no way to see the run die at 0:30 rather than at 10 minutes. The 10 / 12.6 / 18-minute figures were never durations. They were just when somebody happened to LOOK at a corpse that had been cold since 0:30. HONEST ACCOUNTING: I told Ivan 'fixed' three times. Each fix was real and each was necessary, but I twice let a plausible explanation stand in for a verified one, and never once checked the platform's own limit on the primitive the whole architecture rests on. The pattern to break: when a symptom survives a fix, re-derive from first principles instead of reaching for the next plausible bug. README's 'Does it work while logged out? Partly - a generation you started completes without you (ctx.waitUntil)' was FALSE and has been for the life of the project. RETRY DANGER: Workflows auto-retries steps by default. On a 19k-token call that is a silent double-spend. The LLM step must pin retries to 0; only the D1 save may retry.

tasks.title: A tab left open across a deploy silently runs the OLD front-end — this cost a full debugging round
tasks.description: Ivan 2026-07-16: 'I never saw a progress bar, I still saw a spinning circle.' The progress bar WAS deployed (verified: the served /app.js contained progress-wrap). But the app is a single-page app served from a plain /app.js with no version query, so a tab opened before the deploy keeps running the JS already in memory — clicking Generate ran the old code. The spinner he saw had been deleted from the source. This is not cosmetic: it made a shipped fix look like a failed fix, and sent us both chasing the wrong thing during an already-confusing four-round debugging session.
acceptance_criteria: The app can tell that the deployed version differs from the loaded one
The user is told to reload (or it reloads itself) rather than silently running stale code
The check costs nothing extra — the front end already polls, so it should ride on an existing response
Works without a build step (assets are served statically, there is no bundler to hash filenames)
testing_criteria: Load the page, deploy a new version, confirm the running tab notices within one poll
Confirm no reload prompt appears when the version is unchanged
Confirm a failed version check never breaks the app (same rule as logEvent)
notes: Cheap to fix and it protects every future debugging session: without it, 'is this deployed?' and 'is this working?' are indistinguishable from the user's side. Simplest approach that needs no bundler: stamp a build id into the Worker, return it on an existing polled endpoint (e.g. /api/me or the calls list), and have app.js compare it to the id it booted with. On mismatch, show a non-blocking 'A new version is available - reload' toast. Do NOT auto-reload while a generation is in flight without warning. Related: cache-control on /app.js is already 'max-age=0, must-revalidate', so a plain reload DOES fetch fresh code - the problem is exclusively the never-reloaded tab. NEW FINDING 2026-07-17 (measured, not theorised): for ~2-3 minutes after a deploy, Cloudflare's edge serves a MIX of the old and new asset across colos - both reporting cf-cache-status: HIT. Measured right after a deploy: 3 of 5 requests got the STALE app.js; it converged to 8/8 new after ~3 min. Two consequences. (1) A post-deploy grep of the live asset can report GREEN on the old file and RED on the new one purely by luck - my own verification lied twice tonight. Always re-check ~3 min after deploy, or send Cache-Control: no-cache which bypasses the edge and returns the true current asset. (2) This is a SECOND way the user can see stale code, independent of the never-reloaded tab: a reload during the propagation window can genuinely fetch old JS. It self-heals, so it does not change this task's fix (a build-id check), but it does mean 'reload and it'll be fine' is not always immediately true.
test_results: 2026-07-17 /api/me returns build id = git SHA, injected via `wrangler deploy --var BUILD_ID:${{github.sha}}`; BUILD_ID confirmed live on the worker (6c3ad711...). app.js remembers the boot build and polls /me every 60s; on a changed build it shows a non-blocking 'A new version is available — Reload' banner. Browser-verified: flipping the stubbed build id raised the banner with a working Reload button. 'dev' (manual deploy) stays quiet.

tasks.title: Retry transient LLM failures — the first autonomous cron run died on overloaded_error and would never have retried
tasks.description: Found 2026-07-17 by the FIRST cron run, which otherwise worked perfectly: 07:10:09 imported a call and started generation with nobody logged in, exactly one attempt (no double-spend), then 07:10:19 generation.failed 'Anthropic stream error: overloaded_error — Overloaded' after 8.6s. That is a 529 — Anthropic's docs say retry it with backoff, and the official SDKs do so automatically. We call fetch directly AND pinned the Workflow step to retries:{limit:0}, so it failed permanently. Gabriel records early; if Anthropic is busy at 6am every call fails and sits there until a human clicks Regenerate, and no human is awake. The zero-retry guard was too blunt: it correctly stops re-spending on a run that already did the work, but an overload at 8.6s had generated nothing.
acceptance_criteria: Transient failures retry with bounded exponential backoff
Permanent failures are NEVER retried — a bad key must not be tried four times
The Workflow step stays at retries:0; retry the individual API call, not the whole 4-call generation
Retries are logged, so a run that succeeded on attempt 3 is not indistinguishable from a clean one
A permanent outage gives up rather than looping
testing_criteria: Simulate the exact mid-stream overloaded_error and assert recovery
Assert 401/400/403 are tried exactly once
Assert a refusal is not retried
Assert the retry ceiling and that backoff actually waits
tasks.notes: CLASSIFICATION IS THE WHOLE POINT. Transient: HTTP 408/409/429/500/502/503/504/529, network errors, our own AbortSignal timeout, and mid-stream overloaded_error/api_error/rate_limit_error/timeout_error. Permanent: 400/401/403, refusals, stop_reason max_tokens, malformed JSON — these fail identically every time and retrying only burns money. TRAP CAUGHT IN REVIEW: my first refactor wrapped the whole Anthropic fetch+classify in one try/catch, which would have converted a permanent 401 into a TransientError and retried a bad key 4x. The catch now covers ONLY the network call. COST: a retry re-sends input, so it is not free — justified because the failures we retry die early, and the alternative is silent overnight failure.

tasks.title: Generation fails on raw control chars in model JSON (John Vachalek call)
tasks.description: Ivan 2026-07-17: John Vachalek call showed 'Last attempt failed. Bad control character in string literal in JSON at position 1547'. The log shows generation.debrief_done at 68s SUCCEEDED, then generation.failed at 76.6s — so the debrief parsed and a TONE DRAFT did not. The model emitted literal newlines inside the email body instead of escaped \n; strict JSON.parse rejects raw control chars (0x00-0x1F) in strings. Cost: a full 76s generation thrown away over an unescaped newline, with the call left 'failed'.

Both parse sites (debrief and tone) use the tolerant parser

tasks.notes: parseModelJson: strict JSON.parse first; ONLY on a /control character/ error, walk the string (tracking in-string state and backslash escapes) escaping raw control chars, then retry. Any other error rethrows so a real breakage isn't masked. A parser walk, not a blind regex, so only chars inside string literals are touched. This is a general model-output-hygiene fix and protects every future call, not just this one. 15/15 unit tests.

tasks.title: PARKED ON A BRANCH — Fathom API returns the WHOLE ORG's recordings; scope polling with recorded_by[]
tasks.description: 2026-07-17/18: Gabriel reported 'our ai is picking up on other peoples calls'. Dusty Gulleson met with a DIFFERENT rep at the company and her call landed in our inbox; we also got multiple copies of one group call. ROOT CAUSE (confirmed in Fathom's own API docs): GET /external/v1/meetings 'returns team and workspace recordings (not solely the API key owner's)'. tasks.title: Spend windows in Activity: today / last 7 days / this month / all time
tasks.description: Ivan asked for Anthropic balance plus spend today/week/lifetime in Activity, and whether the API key can see it. Researched against current Anthropic docs (2026-07-19). Findings: (1) ACCOUNT/CREDIT BALANCE IS NOT EXPOSED BY ANY ANTHROPIC API — the Admin API covers members, workspaces, keys, usage and cost reports only; balance is Console-only. (2) Real billed spend IS available via GET /v1/organizations/cost_report and /v1/organizations/usage_report/messages, but requires a separate ADMIN API KEY (sk-ant-admin01-...), not the sk-ant-api key we store; the Admin API is unavailable to individual accounts (org must exist in Console > Settings > Organization); cost_report is 1d granularity, USD, ~5min lag. NOT ADOPTED: an admin key can list and deactivate API keys and manage org members, and our D1 stores keys in plaintext by design — that is Ivan's call to make deliberately, not a default. (3) Today/week/lifetime from our OWN events log needed no new key and shipped here.

tasks.title: Release-notes popup, once per browser per release
tasks.description: Ivan and Gabriel share one login and work remotely, so 'has this person read the notes' cannot be stored on the account row — one of them dismissing it would silence the other. The marker lives in localStorage, which is per-browser, so each of them gets it once on their own machine on the first refresh after an update. Keyed on a hand-written release version (RELEASES[0].v), NOT on BUILD_ID: BUILD_ID is the git SHA and changes on every deploy including typo fixes, and a popup that fires for a one-line CSS change trains you to dismiss it unread. Also fixes a live regression: the previous manual `wrangler deploy` omitted --var BUILD_ID, so BUILD_ID was 'dev' in production and startVersionWatch (TASK-046) had silently stopped polling for new versions. Deploying through CI restores it.

Never renders over the sign-in screen
releasenotes.test.mjs (27 assertions) and appboot.test.mjs pass
tasks.notes: To publish notes for a future release: prepend an entry to RELEASES in public/app.js (newest FIRST — appending to the end silently shows nothing forever; releasenotes.test.mjs asserts the ordering). Deploy via git push so CI sets BUILD_ID; a direct `wrangler deploy` without --var BUILD_ID breaks the stale-tab banner.

tasks.title: CloserAI rename, brand-as-home, collapsible macOS-style sidebar, mobile menu escape
tasks.description: Rename to CloserAI in the header, sign-in card, page title and mobile bar. The brand is now a real <button> that returns to All Calls from anywhere (it drives the actual All Calls nav item rather than showCallsView(), which only picks a filter when none is active and so would have stranded you on Closed). Desktop sidebar collapses via a toggle beside the list title, persisted per browser. Sidebar restyled as an inset rounded panel per macOS Tahoe — explicitly NOT glassy, no backdrop-filter (a blur layer costs a compositor pass on every scroll). ROOT CAUSE of the mobile trap, found by rendering the app locally rather than by reading the code: #navScrim shipped as class='nav-scrim hidden', and .hidden is `display:none !important`, while the JS only ever toggled `.show`. The !important always won, so the scrim was NEVER visible or clickable on ANY device since it was written — tap-outside-to-close had never worked, which is why a full page reload was the only escape. My first diagnosis (iOS Safari dropping click on non-interactive elements without cursor:pointer) was WRONG as the cause; cursor:pointer was kept as defence but it was not the bug.
acceptance_criteria: App reads CloserAI in header, sign-in card, <title> and mobile bar
Brand click returns to All Calls even when Closed/Archived is the active filter

The menu can still be REOPENED after the content-tap dismisser was added (no new trap)
Sidebar collapse persists per browser and is neutralised inside the mobile media query
Sidebar has rounded corners and is inset; no backdrop-filter anywhere
nav.test.mjs (33 assertions) passes AND fails when the `hidden` class is reintroduced
Verified in a real browser at 1280x800 and 375x812, not only by unit test
tasks.notes: LESSON: the first version of nav.test.mjs asserted classList state and passed with the bug fully present — every class was correct, only the computed style was wrong. Class-level assertions cannot see `display:none !important`. The bug was found by serving public/ with a stubbed API (scratchpad/uiserver.mjs) and reading getComputedStyle in a real browser. For any future UI state bug, render it; do not reason about it from the source. The suite now asserts the markup directly and was verified to fail when the bug is reintroduced.

project_principles: DECISION AUTHORITY (Ivan, 2026-07-19): Ivan is the ultimate authority on what gets built and deployed. Gabriel's input from calls is SUGGESTION and raw material, not specification — capture it on the potential-features list, but do not treat it as approved work. Ivan's words: 'I trust my instincts and design skills more than his... take it with a grain of salt, though I do expect that he will have great ideas.' PRACTICALLY: never move a Gabriel idea into a build round without Ivan asking for it; when Gabriel's suggestion conflicts with Ivan's design judgement, Ivan wins; surface Gabriel's ideas as options with tradeoffs rather than as requirements.
